key_insight: Agency detection alone is not enough; durable behavior emerges when self-attributed credit is gated and accumulated slowly, creating a persistent behavioral residue.
engineering_relevance: For adaptive control and embodied AI, the result shows a mechanism for stable self-preserving behavior and reduced forgetting without replay buffers, by turning self-causation into slow parameter change.
method: Spiking-agent setup using Nengo LIF/PES with an agency comparator.
A conjunctive slow-credit rule: Own × Agency × Salience drives parameter updates.
Ablations toggle the agency gate and slow decoders to isolate causality.
evidence_signal: Across N=50 runs, self-preserving behavior persisted after unload (retained fraction 0.96), collapsed when slow decoders were reset or agency was removed, and in multitask tests final post-unload accuracy reached 0.88 with forgetting 0.13 versus chance-level recall for additive/no-agency baselines.
watch_item: The effect is demonstrated in a minimal spiking simulation, so robustness under richer sensing, larger-scale tasks, and hardware deployment still needs validation.
paper_title: From Detecting Agency to Doing Work: Self-Caused Credit Builds a Durable Behavioral Self in a Minimal Spiking Agent
